title: IaC: Why Terraform Exists
subtitle: Commercial before the movie: why IaC became necessary

nodes.label: Before IaC
nodes.definition: Before Infrastructure as Code, infrastructure was often created manually, documented loosely, or automated with scattered scripts.
nodes.whyItMatters: This made environments harder to repeat, review, audit, and recover.
nodes.safeSentence: Before IaC, infrastructure changes often depended too much on manual steps and tribal knowledge.
nodes.commonTrap: Do not assume every old environment was bad. The problem was repeatability and scale.
nodes.interviewAnswer: IaC became important because manual and loosely scripted infrastructure did not scale well as cloud environments became larger and more dynamic.

nodes.label: Manual Console Work
nodes.definition: Manual console work means creating or changing infrastructure by clicking through a cloud provider UI.
nodes.whyItMatters: It may be fast once, but it is hard to repeat exactly, review in advance, or track over time.
nodes.safeSentence: Manual console work can be useful for learning, but it becomes risky as a main team workflow.
nodes.commonTrap: Do not say the console is useless. It is useful, but not enough as the main system of record.
nodes.interviewAnswer: The console is helpful for exploration, but teams usually want repeatable, version-controlled infrastructure changes.

nodes.label: Scripts and Tribal Knowledge
nodes.definition: Scripts automate steps, while tribal knowledge is undocumented knowledge held by a few people.
nodes.whyItMatters: Scripts can help, but without a consistent model they may become hard to understand, reuse, or audit.
nodes.safeSentence: Scripts are useful, but IaC gives teams a more structured way to describe infrastructure.
nodes.commonTrap: Do not treat all scripts as bad. The issue is whether the process is reliable, reviewable, and shared.
nodes.interviewAnswer: IaC improves on scattered scripts by giving teams a clearer desired-state model and a shared source of truth.

nodes.label: Cloud Growth Problem
nodes.definition: The cloud growth problem happens when environments grow too large and dynamic for manual setup to stay safe.
nodes.whyItMatters: More accounts, regions, services, teams, and environments create more chances for mistakes and drift.
nodes.safeSentence: As cloud environments grow, teams need infrastructure changes to be repeatable, reviewable, and controlled.
nodes.commonTrap: Do not think IaC is only for huge companies. Even small teams benefit from repeatability.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Cloud scale made manual infrastructure management harder, which is one reason IaC became so important.

nodes.label: Infrastructure as Code
nodes.definition: Infrastructure as Code means defining infrastructure in files that can be versioned, reviewed, reused, and automated.
nodes.whyItMatters: IaC turns infrastructure changes into an engineering workflow instead of only a manual operations task.
nodes.safeSentence: IaC makes infrastructure more repeatable, reviewable, and easier to manage as a team.
nodes.commonTrap: IaC does not remove the need for human judgment. Bad code can still create bad infrastructure.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Infrastructure as Code lets teams manage cloud resources through reviewed, version-controlled definitions instead of relying only on manual changes.

note.summary: Simple landscape from pre-IaC pain to Terraform and alternatives.

image.caption: IaC landscape flow from pre-IaC pain points to Terraform and alternatives.

nodes.label: Terraform
nodes.definition: Terraform is a popular IaC tool that uses providers and a desired-state workflow to manage infrastructure across many platforms.
nodes.whyItMatters: It gives teams one common workflow for AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, Kubernetes, SaaS platforms, and observability tools.
nodes.safeSentence: Terraform is popular because it gives teams a consistent IaC workflow across many platforms.
nodes.commonTrap: Do not say Terraform is the only IaC option. It is one major option in a larger tool landscape.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Terraform is often chosen because it is platform-flexible, provider-based, and built around plan, apply, and state.

nodes.label: CloudFormation
nodes.definition: CloudFormation is AWS-native Infrastructure as Code that uses templates to define AWS resources as stacks.
nodes.whyItMatters: It is deeply integrated with AWS and is a common comparison point when talking about Terraform.
nodes.safeSentence: CloudFormation is strong for AWS-native infrastructure, while Terraform is often used when teams want a broader multi-platform workflow.
nodes.commonTrap: Do not call CloudFormation obsolete. It is still important in AWS environments.
nodes.interviewAnswer: If a team is all-in on AWS, CloudFormation can make sense; Terraform is often chosen when teams want a consistent workflow across more than AWS.

nodes.label: Ansible
nodes.definition: Ansible is an automation tool often used for configuration management, deployment, orchestration, and some provisioning tasks.
nodes.whyItMatters: It overlaps with IaC but is usually discussed more as automation and configuration management than pure Terraform-style desired-state provisioning.
nodes.safeSentence: Ansible is useful automation, especially for configuration and operational tasks, while Terraform is usually stronger for declarative infrastructure provisioning.
nodes.commonTrap: Do not treat Ansible and Terraform as identical tools. They overlap, but their sweet spots are different.
nodes.interviewAnswer: I think of Terraform as infrastructure provisioning and Ansible as broader automation/configuration management, though they can overlap.

nodes.label: Pulumi
nodes.definition: Pulumi is an IaC tool that lets teams define infrastructure using general programming languages such as Python, TypeScript, Go, C#, Java, and others.
nodes.whyItMatters: It appeals to teams that want to use familiar programming languages instead of Terraform HCL.
nodes.safeSentence: Pulumi is another IaC option, especially attractive when teams prefer general programming languages.
nodes.commonTrap: Do not say Pulumi is just Terraform with another syntax. It has a different programming model and ecosystem.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Pulumi is an alternative IaC tool that lets teams define infrastructure using regular programming languages rather than only a domain-specific language.

nodes.label: OpenTofu
nodes.definition: OpenTofu is a Terraform-compatible open-source fork created after HashiCorp changed Terraform licensing.
nodes.whyItMatters: It matters because some teams care deeply about open-source governance, compatibility, and vendor control.
nodes.safeSentence: OpenTofu is important to know because it lives in the Terraform ecosystem and is often discussed as an open-source alternative.
nodes.commonTrap: Do not present OpenTofu as a completely unrelated tool. It is closely tied to the Terraform ecosystem.
nodes.interviewAnswer: OpenTofu is a Terraform-compatible fork that some teams consider when they want a more open-source governance model.

paths.label: IaC Landscape Walk
paths.description: Walk from pre-IaC pain points to IaC and key Terraform-neighborhood alternatives.
paths.rehearsalPrompt: Explain why IaC emerged, why Terraform became popular, and what alternatives people mention.
